The primary requirements of the process are to recover the unburned fuel $(^{233}\mathrm{UF}_4)$ and fuel-stream carrier salts (LiF-BeF $_2$ ) from the fuel stream, and the LiF-ThF $_4$ plus the bred $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ from the fertile stream.
In this study, the plant capacity is based upon treating 15 ft $^3$ /day of fuel salt and 105 ft $^3$ /day of fertile salt removed as side streams.
These capacities are adequate for a 1000-Mw (electrical) power reactor.
It is only necessary to remove the bred $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ sufficiently fast to keep a low concentration in the blanket, thereby ensuring a low fission rate and negligible

The chief conclusions of this study are: (1) that the fluorination-distillation process for the fuel stream and the fluorination process for the fertile stream comprise a compact and relatively simple system that can be engineered with a normal amount of developmental work, and (2) that integration of the processing plant into the reactor facility is both feasible and economical and the logical way to take advantage of the processing possibilities of a fluid-fueled reactor.
These costs contribute about 0.2 mill/kwhr to the fuel cycle cost when the reactor operates at an $80\%$ plant factor and capital charges are amortized at $10\%$ year.

For this study, the stream compositions are: (1) a fuel stream consisting of an LiF-BeF $_2$ (69-31 mole %) carrier that contains the fissile component, and (2) a fertile stream, which surrounds the fuel stream, consisting of a 71-29 mole % LiF-ThF $_4$ mixture.
In each stream the lithium is about 99.995 at.% $^{7}$ Li.

The above compositions are not unique; other MSBR designs might use different compositions. $^{39}$ The configuration of the system allows a relatively high neutron leakage rate from the fuel stream into the surrounding fertile stream, where capture by thorium breeds additional $^{233}$ U fuel.
The reactor is operated at an average temperature of about $650^{\circ}$ C; fission energy is recovered in external heat exchangers through which the fuel and fertile streams are circulated.
As mentioned above, the fuel is $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , and one carrier component is the highly enriched $^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ isotope.
A modest vacuum of only 1 to $2\mathrm{mmHg}$ is required, but the temperature (about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) is higher than any normally encountered in handling molten fluoride salts.
The operation was explored first by Kelley<sup>3</sup> in laboratory experiments that indicated LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub> decontamination from fission products by factors of $10^{2}$ to $10^{3}$ .
Fission products can be concentrated in the still residue (primarily LiF) by a factor of about 250 by using the decay heat of the fission products to volatilize LiF-BeF<sub>2</sub>.
The corresponding increase in the rate of decay heat generation limited the cycle time to about 68 days.

The reaction is carried out by absorbing $\mathsf{UF}_6$ in a molten mixture of LiF-BeF $_2$ - $\mathsf{UF}_4$ at about $600^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ followed by contacting with $\mathsf{H}_2$ to reduce $\mathsf{UF}_6$ in situ.
This is a conceptual design<sup>1</sup> of a power reactor capable of producing 1000 Mw (electrical) with a thermal efficiency of $45\%$ .
Graphite occupies about 79 vol % of the core, fuel salt about 15 vol %, and fertile salt about 6.
The core is surrounded radially and axially by a 3.5-ft blanket of LiF-ThF<sub>4</sub> mixture, and the blanket is in turn surrounded by a 6-in.-thick graphite reflector.
The core is about 8 ft in diameter and 17 ft high; overall, the reactor plus breeder blanket is about 16 ft in diameter and 25 ft high.

A large amount of data is available for each step except for the distillation and reduction operations, both of which have been demonstrated in the laboratory.[3,26] However, this study does assume the necessary engineering extrapolations to convert from the current batchwise operations to continuous operations.
To accomplish this, the stream is required to go through the processing plant on a relatively short cycle, for example, once every 20 to 50 days.
The cycle time for this study is 22 days.
A 30-year discard cycle suffices.
A cycle time of 40 to 70 days is required to maintain the fission-product concentration at a low enough level for attractive breeding performance.
The facilities are designed for a 30-year capacity and are located underground a short distance from the chemical processing area.
Interim storage of 5-year duration is provided for these solids.
A maximum of about $6.5\%$ of the total energy of the system is associated with beta and gamma energy in the fission products; this amounts to about 140 Mw (thermal) in the MSBR fuel stream.
Most of this energy is emitted quickly, decreasing about $82\%$ in 1 hr and $95\%$ in 1 day.

The blanket salt needs no further purification, and a portion of the $^{233}\mathrm{UF}$ is sent to the fuel stream by dissolving it in the carrier salt and reducing it with hydrogen.
The other interactions are physical and require only heat and mass transport; however, in the case of distillation, a rather high temperature (about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) is involved.
Currently the process is in the pilot plant phase at ORNL.[17,18] One of the steps in this process is batch fluorination of the molten fluorides to remove uranium hexafluoride.
As noted before, uranium in these streams is in the form of $\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ .
Contact times (fluorine and molten salt) of 1 to 2 hr have been shown to be adequate for uranium removal down to 10 ppm in such batch tests.

It is difficult to predict the fluorine utilization in a continuous fluorinator; however, it should be better than that for batch fluorination, which suggests that a utilization of $33 - 1 / 3\%$ will probably be achieved.

Molybdenum fluoride is removed from the UF $_6$ by isolating the $100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ NaF zone, desorbing the UF $_6$ and molybdenum fluoride (by raising the temperature to $>150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and passing fluorine through the bed), and passing desorbed gas through a fixed bed of $\mathrm{MgF}_2$ , which is held at about $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Such a system has been used effectively in pilot plant operations, and a similar system would be desirable for the MSBR processing facility.[17,18] In our concept, the system has a movable bed of NaF pellets, and, after each sorption cycle, some of the lower NaF is mechanically ejected to waste.
As the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ leaves the last sorption trap it must be collected and ultimately used as feed for the fuel-reconstitution step.
This is done by collecting the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ in cold traps.
The first, or primary trap, is operated at about $-40^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the second, or backup trap, is operated at about $-60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Conventional designs are available for such units in which there are internal cooling fins for collecting $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ , and Calrod heaters for vaporizing the $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ for removal (Fig.

This calls for at least three primary cold traps for continuous operation: one for collecting $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ , one for feeding $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{6}$ , and one in transition between these two functions.
Since there is excess fluorine in the fluorinator off-gas (33-1/3% utilization), fluorine is recycled.
This recycle contains some fission products, so it is necessary to remove a side-stream (10%) to prevent their buildup.

At the average operating temperature (about $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) of the MSBR itself, the solubility of rare-earth fluorides in fuel salt is only a few mole percent; however, at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the solubility in LiF alone is about 50 mole % for the more insoluble compounds, for example, $\mathrm{LaF}_3$ , $\mathrm{PrF}_3$ , and $\mathrm{CeF}_3$ .

Physics calculations on the 58-day-cycle MSBR indicate that at equilibrium the molar ratio of LiF: $(\mathrm{RE})\mathrm{F}_3$ in the fuel is about 1400, a number considerably greater than the 1:l ratio permitted by the solubility limit at $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

It is therefore apparent that, based on solubility data alone, distillation at about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ can tolerate an extremely large rare-earth concentration factor before precipitation occurs.

Four waste streams requiring storage leave the processing facility: (1) NaF and $\mathrm{MgF}_2$ sorbent from the UF $_6$ purification system, (2) aqueous waste from the KOH scrubber, (3) molten-salt residue from the distillation unit, and (4) molten salt from fertile stream discard.

Spent NaF and $\mathrm{MgF_2}$ pellets, which retain the volatile fission and corrosion product fluorides from the UF gas stream, are stored in 10-in.-diam by 8-ft-high stainless steel cylinders in a concrete vault adjacent to the still-residue waste tank.

The vault is designed to contain a 5-year collection of cylinders (160 cylinders at $100\%$ plant factor); after 5 years, the older cylinders are removed and transferred to a permanent underground storage site.
The integrated heat generation when the vault contains 160 cylinders is about $1.73 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{Btu/hr}$ .
Forced circulation of about 11,300 scfm of air at a temperature rise of $125^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is used to remove this energy.

Residue from the vacuum still is stored in bulk in a facility similar to one evaluated previously by Carter and Ruch. $^{14}$ A single, large tank equipped with adequate cooling tubes and adequate for a 30-year accumulation of waste is provided.

One of the major goals in this design study was to estimate the cost of an integrated facility for processing the fuel and blanket salts for this conceptual 1000-Mw (electrical) MSBR.
The estimate arrived at includes the total fixed capital cost and the annual operating cost which are, respectively, $5,301,510 and $787,790 per year.
In general, conventional estimating practices were used except where past experience in the nuclear energy field indicated changes.[29-32]

Briefly, the system consists in (1) fluorinating, distilling, and reconstituting the molten fluorides used in the reactor core, and (2) recovering the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ from the molten breeder blanket by fluorination and using the uranium to reconstitute the core salt.
The power of the breeder reactor was set at 1,000 Mw (electrical) for this study.

The relatively small size of this sidestream processing plant, about 12 ft³ salt per day for a 1000-Mw (electrical) reactor, is amenable to integrated construction, thereby separating the economic dependence of the processing industry upon a large amount of installed electrical capacity.
The estimated capital cost, excluding inventory, of the plant is $5,301,000, and operating costs are about $788,000 a year.
It is significant that the capital investment in the integrated processing plant proposed here is only about 4% of the total cost of the reactor system it serves.

The still temperature of about $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is much higher than has been contemplated for any other part of the MSBR system, and the resistance of INOR-8 and nickel to corrosive attack at this temperature is not known.
The reactor fuel system contains about 650 ft³ of salt, and, without continuous fluorination, the out-of-pile inventory could possibly be as large as the in-pile inventory.
This study indicates that in a continuous fluorination-distillation process the holdup represents about $10\%$ of the reactor fuel volume.
This concept calls for a $1/2$ - to $3/4$ -in.-thick layer of frozen salt on the inside wall of the fluorinator to prevent the serious corrosive attack by the molten mixture during fluorination.
However, fertile-stream processing rates are 8 to 10 times higher, and the fission-product activity is several orders of magnitude less.
The theoretical net discard of ${}^7\mathrm{Li}$ in the still waste is low enough that its cost is insignificant compared with other fuel cycle costs.

Relative volatility data are needed for the multicomponent mixture, LiF-BeF $_2$ -fission products, in which compositions are in the range 99.5-0.5-0 mol % to 84.5-0.5-15 mol % LiF-BeF $_2$ -rare earth fluorides.
However, the UF<sub>6</sub> product that would eventually be handled by contact would need purification.
The calculated loss of $7 \text{Li}$ and $233 \text{U}$ in the fluorination-distillation process is small, being only 1.5 to $2\%$ of the fuel-cycle cost.
The accumulated waste generates about 4.5 Mw (thermal) throughout most of the filling period of the waste tank.

At the end of the 30-year filling period, the waste tank contains about $141,000\mathrm{kg}$ of Th, $10,400\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{7Li}$ , and $116\mathrm{kg}$ of $233\mathrm{U}$ ; the $233\mathrm{U}$ is isotopically pure material, having been formed almost entirely out of the fission zone.

Although not considered in this study, an in-cell decay period of about six months followed by refluorination appears to be advisable for greatly reducing the amount of $233\mathrm{Pa}$ that enters the waste tank.

Two principal conditions had to be satisfied in the still design: (1) the evaporating surface had to be sufficient for obtaining the required 15-ft³/day distillation rate, and (2) maximum heat transfer surface had to be provided to minimize the frequency with which the still is drained to the waste tank.

The choice of dimensions was somewhat arbitrary; a tube-sheet diameter of 2.5 ft was chosen because it gave an evaporating surface that was about 2.5 times the calculated area, and a closely spaced arrangement of 2.5-ft-long by 1/2-in.-diam tubes was used for high cooling-surface-to-volume ratio.
D-3 depicts the still operation and summarizes calculated performance and physical data.

The calculations which are described below indicate that the still can accumulate fission products for about 67.4 days in a $4\text{-ft}^3$ LiF pool when the still is operated continuously at a feed rate of 15 ft³/day.
The discard rate for LiF is therefore a very small fraction of the processing rate, being about $0.4\%$ .

Heat Removed by Still Condenser. - The load on the still condenser is relatively small, consisting only of the latent heat in 15 ft $^3$ /day of a 69-31 mole % LiF-BeF $_2$ mixture plus some radiation from the pool surface.
The latent heat of vaporization of LiF was estimated from vapor pressure data to be 44,000 cal/g-mole; an experimental value $^{11}$ of 50,100 cal/g-mole was used for BeF $_2$ .
Radiative heat transfer amounted to about 62,700 Btu/hr, giving a total condenser duty of 198,300 Btu/hr.

For smooth, nonflashing distillation, the condensate is the LiF-BeF $_2$ eutectic having a melting point about $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and a condenser temperature slightly higher than this is satisfactory.

If there are deviations from ideal operation so that a higher-melting composition distills, the condenser temperature could be adjusted to temperatures slightly above the melting points of the pure components, $803^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for BeF $_2$ and $845^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for LiF.
